operators-and-expressions.label: Operators and Expressions
operators-and-expressions.summary: Combine values with arithmetic, comparison, and boolean operators.

sketch0.title: Expressions combine values into new results

sketch1.title: Comparison and boolean operators produce True or False

sketch2.title: Python follows an order when evaluating expressions

sc-arithmetic-expression.title: Identify an arithmetic expression
sc-arithmetic-expression.prompt: Which line is an arithmetic expression that evaluates to the number 8?
sc-arithmetic-expression.hint: Look for an operation with numbers that produces 8.

help.concept: An arithmetic expression uses operators like +, -, *, or / to combine numbers into a new numeric value.
help.hint_1: Check each option by mentally evaluating it.
help.hint_2: Only one option produces a numeric result of 8.

sc-parentheses-order.title: Use parentheses to change the result

help.concept: Parentheses tell Python to evaluate part of an expression before the rest of the operators.
help.hint_1: Find the value inside the parentheses before multiplying.
help.hint_2: After adding the two numbers, multiply that result by 4.

mc-boolean-results.title: Choose all expressions that are True
mc-boolean-results.prompt: Which expressions evaluate to `True`? Choose all correct answers.
mc-boolean-results.hint: Test each comparison or boolean expression one by one.

help.concept: Comparison operators produce True or False, and boolean operators combine those results.
help.hint_1: Check the comparisons first, then apply `and` or `or`.
help.hint_2: More than one option is correct, so do not stop after finding the first true expression.

fb-string-plus.title: Complete a string expression
fb-string-plus.prompt: Choose the operator that joins the two strings.
fb-string-plus.hint: In beginner Python, one operator is commonly used to join strings.

help.concept: Python can combine two strings into one larger string using string concatenation.
help.hint_1: Think about the same symbol used for adding numbers and joining strings.
help.hint_2: The correct choice is the operator that places the text together into one result.

code-output-expressions.title: Print expression results
code-output-expressions.prompt: Write Python code that prints the result of these three expressions on separate lines, in this order: `2 + 3`, `7 > 10`, and `(4 == 4) and (1 < 2)`.

help.concept: Python evaluates expressions first, then `print` shows the resulting value in the output panel.
help.hint_1: You need three output lines, so use three print calls.
help.hint_2: The second and third lines should print boolean results from comparison and boolean expressions.

code-output-expressions.starterCode: # Print the result of each expression on its own line

what-is-an-expression.title: Expressions combine values into new results
what-is-an-expression.bodyMarkdown:
An **expression** is code that Python can evaluate to produce a value. Operators are the symbols that help combine values, such as `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `==`, and `and`.

Here is a simple example:

```python
print(2 + 3)
print(10 - 4)
print(3 * 2)
```

Step by step:
- `2 + 3` becomes `5`
- `10 - 4` becomes `6`
- `3 * 2` becomes `6`
- `print(...)` shows each result in the output panel

You can also combine strings with `+`:

```python
print("Hello, " + "Zoe")
```

This joins the two strings and prints `Hello, Zoe`.

Try it yourself: Type one of these examples in the code editor, click Run, and then change one value to see how the output changes.

comparison-and-boolean.title: Comparison and boolean operators produce True or False
comparison-and-boolean.bodyMarkdown:
Comparison operators check whether something is true. For example, `==` means “is equal to” and `>` means “is greater than.” Boolean operators like `and`, `or`, and `not` combine or change true/false results.

Example:

```python
print(5 > 3)
print(5 == 3)
print((5 > 3) and (2 < 4))
```

Line by line:
- `5 > 3` is `True`
- `5 == 3` is `False`
- `(5 > 3) and (2 < 4)` is `True and True`, so the result is `True`

These expressions are useful when you want Python to check conditions.

Your turn: Change one comparison so the final line becomes `False`. Click Run and check the output panel.

order-of-operations.title: Python follows an order when evaluating expressions
order-of-operations.bodyMarkdown:
When an expression has more than one operator, Python follows an order of operations. Parentheses are a clear way to control that order.

Example:

```python
print(2 + 3 * 4)
print((2 + 3) * 4)
```

Step by step:
- In `2 + 3 * 4`, Python multiplies first: `3 * 4` becomes `12`, then adds `2`, so the result is `14`
- In `(2 + 3) * 4`, Python evaluates the parentheses first: `2 + 3` becomes `5`, then multiplies by `4`, so the result is `20`

This is why parentheses matter: they can change the result.

Try this: Type both lines in the code editor, click Run, and then replace `4` with another number to compare the new outputs.
